currentUse: Private residential condominiums — an 11-unit complex marketed as "The Gables" (The Gables at Wooster Square) at 329 Greene Street, facing Wooster Square Park, New Haven, CT 06511, with underground parking and elevator; units trade regularly on the open market.
summary: The former Šv. Kazimiero (St. Casimir's) Lithuanian church building on Greene Street in New Haven's Wooster Square is standing and fully converted to residential condominiums. The 1872 Victorian Gothic edifice was built as the Davenport Congregational Church (architect Rufus G. Russell), later housed an Italian Baptist congregation, and was purchased in 1927 by the local Lithuanian congregation, which re-established it as St. Casimir Catholic Church at 339 Greene Street. The Organ Historical Society's Pipe Organ Database records that the church closed in 2002 and its 1874 Hook & Hastings organ (Opus 750) was moved in 2003 to St. Francis Roman Catholic Church in New Haven. The building was converted into an 11-unit condominium complex known as "The Gables" — public records give a condo year-built of 2006, units were on the MLS by September 2006, and the New Haven Independent (2018) credits local architect George Buchanan with the conversion, which Wooster Square neighbors repeatedly praised as a model of sensitive historic reuse. Global True Lithuania notes the reconstruction preserved the front facade, including the crosses, but reports that nothing visibly Lithuanian remains. The condos now sell at premium prices: unit 8 sold for $720,000 in July 2024 and unit 6 for $695,000, and a March 2026 architectural-history profile confirms the building remains residential within a local historic district. No Lithuanian community activity, reunions, or preservation campaigns connected to the New Haven parish were found in recent coverage.

developments.headline: Parish closed; historic organ relocated in 2003
developments.detail: The Organ Historical Society's Pipe Organ Database entry for the church's E. & G.G. Hook & Hastings organ (Opus 750, 1874) states: 'Church closed in 2002. Organ moved in 2003 to Saint Francis Roman Catholic Church, New Haven, Connecticut.' An earlier database note records that as of 1998 the church was already slated for closure. The entry lists the church address as 339 Greene Street.

sources.title: E. & G.G. Hook & Hastings (Opus 750, 1874), St. Casimir's Lithuanian Roman Catholic Church, New Haven, CT

developments.headline: Building converted to 11 condominiums, 'The Gables'
developments.detail: New Haven public records list a condo year-built of 2006 for 329 Greene Street, the complex is named 'THE GABLES' with 11 total units, and MLS history shows units first listed in September 2006 at $575,000. Listings describe original stained-glass windows and architectural wood beams retained 'from the original church before its conversion to condos.'

sources.title: 329 Greene St #8, New Haven, CT 06511 — sale, tax, and property history
sources.publisher: Redfin / Smart MLS / New Haven public records

developments.headline: Conversion cited as neighborhood model of historic reuse
developments.detail: Reporting on a proposed conversion of nearby St. Michael's school buildings, the New Haven Independent noted architect George Buchanan's firm 'brought to its conversion of another Wooster Square church, St. Casimir's on Greene Street, into condos a decade ago,' and Wooster Square neighbors 'continually praised Buchanan for his work on the conversion of the former St. Casimir's Church.'

developments.headline: Condo units selling at premium prices
developments.detail: Unit 8 (3BR/2.5BA, 1,780 sq ft) sold July 31, 2024 for $720,000, after prior sales of $455,000 (Jan 2017) and $515,000 (Dec 2020). Unit 6 sold for $695,000 per a LandVest/Seabury Hill listing describing the building as 'Formerly the Davenport Congregational Church (c.1872)' with original stained glass and arched beams throughout.

sources.title: 329 Greene St #8, New Haven, CT 06511 — sale history
329 Greene Street, Unit 6, New Haven CT 06511 (sold listing)
sources.publisher: LandVest / Christie's International Real Estate (Seabury Hill Realtors)

developments.headline: Architectural-history profile confirms building standing as residences
developments.detail: Buildings of New England published a profile of the 'Former St. Casimir's Church (1872),' recounting its origin as Davenport Congregational Church (Rufus G. Russell), the Italian Baptist period, the 1927 purchase by the Lithuanian congregation, and stating that after the congregation closed, developers purchased the property within a local historic district and converted the church into residential units.

sources.title: E. & G.G. Hook & Hastings (Opus 750, 1874), St. Casimir's Lithuanian Roman Catholic Church, New Haven, CT

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Two refinements to the archive record. (1) Closure year: the archive says 'closed ~2001'; the Organ Historical Society's Pipe Organ Database — the most specific source found — states the church closed in 2002 (with the organ moved in 2003), while Global True Lithuania says 2005 (likely an error). 2002 appears to be the best-supported date. (2) Sale/conversion timing and form: the archive says 'building sold ~2007 and converted to condos... still apartments in 2023.' Public records give the condo conversion a year-built of 2006 and units were listed on the MLS by September 2006, so the sale to developers and conversion were complete somewhat earlier than ~2007. The units are owner-occupied condominiums (11-unit 'The Gables' association), not rental apartments — 'still apartments in 2023' should read 'still condominiums.' The ownership/status fields (diocese_rc, closed, diocese_closed) are consistent with everything found.
gaps: Could not verify: (1) the price, buyer, or exact date of the Archdiocese of Hartford's sale of the church property to the condo developer — property-record aggregators only show individual unit resales from 2006 onward, and no press coverage of the original sale was found (searched New Haven Independent, Hartford Courant references, general web); (2) the parish's founding year — sources confirm only that the Lithuanian congregation purchased the 1872 building in 1927; (3) any contemporaneous press about the 2002 closure or final Mass; (4) any present-day Lithuanian community activity, reunions, or commemorations tied to the New Haven parish (none found; a live saintcasimirparish.org site belongs to a different St. Casimir parish, not New Haven). Minor address note: the church was listed at 339 Greene Street while the condos are addressed 329 Greene Street; all sources describe the same Davenport Congregational/St. Casimir building on Greene Street at Wooster Square, but the renumbering itself is not explicitly documented in any source opened.
